query: Do Agents Know When to Stop Instead of Act? alternative

results.title: Teach AI agents when to stop - Fast Company
results.content:
Image 6: Author's image
BYNeel Somani

Audio 1

Listen to this ArticleMore info

0:00 / 0:00

Imagine an AI agent inside your company’s tools, clicking through an interface, moving files, approving a request, or preparing to send money. Now imagine the AI agent hits a case that is ambiguous, high-stakes, or outside of its scope. The question is whether it knows it should stop.

An agent can navigate interfaces and take actions on a user’s behalf. When encountering ambiguity, lacking context, or facing a decision they shouldn’t make, the agents are more likely to make an aggressive error if the system has no good way to say, “I don’t know,” or, more importantly, “I don’t have the authority for this.” [...] That framing is right, but I’d push it one step further. The “abstain state” should be treated like a success state for the right class of problems. When consequences go up, and confidence goes down, the system should stop and hand off.

You can see the failure mode in language models when they work on math problems. Instead of saying, “I don’t know,” they hallucinate. Computer-use agents can do the same thing in a more dangerous form. Without a real fallback, they keep moving through the workflow because continuing is the only behavior available.

That’s why I think execute, escalate, and refuse are the right three states. If those states are not built into the workflow, the system does not have a real way to stop.

## Design the handoff before you deploy [...] 3. What it should refuse entirely: If the request involves sensitive data, a high-consequence decision, or an action the system is not authorized to take, the right behavior is refusal. The same is true when the system would have to guess, improvise, or act outside its role to complete the task.

If that boundary isn’t explicit, the agent will guess. That’s not what you want when the system can click buttons, move money, change records, and delete things.

Put the boundary in the workflow. Make the handoff explicit. Let the agent stop when it should. If the system can’t say, “I don’t have the authority for this,” it isn’t ready.

_Neel Somani is the founder of Eclipse._

Understanding these balances is going to be important to build and use these systems responsibly. So this last key agents plan, act and observe.

results.title: How to Stop AI Agents From Making Mistakes - Agent Prompts Daily
results.content:
add an edge-case stop instead of stretching a check to do a job it can't. [...] How do you stop an AI agent from making the same mistake?  
Write the check into the prompt instead of hoping the agent remembers. Name the exact failure you keep seeing, make the check scan for it on every run, and have the agent stop and flag rather than push through when it hits that case. If a check passed but the mistake still happened, tighten that one item so the same near-miss now fails.

Do you need code or a dataset to evaluate an AI agent?  
No. The whole approach in this guide works with no harness, no dataset, no account, and no code. You paste a block of text at the end of the agent’s instructions. Code-level tools like a grading sub-agent or a pre-action hook are upgrades for later, once you’re running agents at scale. [...] Build context, then stress-test it. Run this when you want the model to think hard instead of agreeing with you. It reads the whole conversation, writes back a shared brief, waits for your confirmation, then attacks your plan from three angles.

results.title: AI agents components and their role in autonomous decision-making
results.content:
Think of an AI agent in a contact center: it might ask a few clever questions, dig through some support docs, and suggest a fix. If the problem’s too tricky, it hands things off to a human, showing it knows when to help and when to take a step back.

## The five core components of AI agents

Infographic showing the five core AI agent components in a loop.

### 1. Perception: How AI takes in the world

AI agents can gather information from their environment, which enables them to understand their surroundings and make well-informed decisions. This ai agent component is responsible for collecting and processing data from various sources, such as: [...] ### How do AI agents work?

AI agents take in information from their surroundings, run it through algorithms, and use that to make decisions based on goals they were given. They act on those decisions, observe the outcomes, and use it to get a better result next time.

They’re not just blindly following orders. They’re built to observe, think, decide, act, and learn. This creates a feedback loop that powers their usefulness across industries like customer support, software development, and data analysis — anywhere fast, smart decisions are needed at scale.

results.title: AI Agents Are Transforming Decision Making: What Leaders Should ...
results.content:
In simple terms:

> _Traditional analytics: human asks → system answers → human decides_
> 
> 
> _AI agents: agent monitors → agent detects → agent analyze → agent actions → human overwrite agent actions if needed & governance_

Here the big shift from being reactive to being proactive is a real game-changer.

## How AI Agents Work: The Core Loop

AI agents run on a loop:

   Trigger — A condition or event initiates the agent’s workflow (for example, new data arriving).

   Agent Action — Agent interprets the situation, decides the next step.

   Iteration — The agent reevaluates the results, adjusts its plan, and continues until the task is complete or a stop condition is reached. This iteration loop is what makes agents smarter and more reliable the longer you use them. [...] Skip to main content
